blocks.body: Present Perfect vs Present Perfect Continuous — Present Perfect = have / has + V3 for a result or fact now, and Present Perfect Continuous = have / has been + V-ing for process, duration, or recent activity up to now.

{"level":"A2–B1","intro":"Present Perfect usually answers what has happened or what is true now because of the action. Present Perfect Continuous usually answers what activity has been going on up to now or how long it has been happening.","rules":[{"label":"PRESENT PERFECT","color":"blue","text":"have / has + V3 for a finished result, experience, or a fact that matters now.","example":"I have lost my keys. / She has already finished."},{"label":"PRESENT PERFECT CONTINUOUS","color":"emerald","text":"have / has been + V-ing for process, duration, or recent activity leading up to now.","example":"I have been looking for my keys for an hour. / She has been working all morning."},{"label":"KEY QUESTION","color":"violet","text":"Ask yourself: what has happened / what is true now? or how long / what has been going on up to now?","example":"finished result now / ongoing or recent activity up to now"}]}

{"title":"1. Quick comparison","intro":"Both tenses connect the past with the present, but the focus is different:","rows":[{"en":"Present Perfect","ua":"a result, experience, or fact now","note":"I have just finished my homework."},{"en":"Present Perfect Continuous","ua":"a process or duration up to the present moment","note":"I have been doing my homework for two hours."},{"en":"just / already / yet","ua":"often point to completion or a ready result now","note":"She has already eaten. / Have you done it yet?"},{"en":"ever / never","ua":"life experience up to now","note":"Have you ever tried sushi? / I have never been there."},{"en":"for / since / how long","ua":"often point to duration and activity up to now","note":"How long have you been waiting?"},{"en":"lately / recently","ua":"often point to recent repeated or ongoing activity","note":"He has been sleeping badly lately."},{"en":"result now vs activity up to now","ua":"have / has + V3 vs have / has been + V-ing","note":"I have painted the door. / I have been painting the door."}],"warning":"If the action is finished and the result matters now, choose Present Perfect. If the activity or duration matters, choose Present Perfect Continuous."}

{"title":"2. Forms grid","intro":"Compare the basic affirmative, negative, and question patterns.","items":[{"label":"Present Perfect","title":"Affirmative","subtitle":"Subject + have / has + V3: I have finished."},{"label":"Present Perfect","title":"Negative","subtitle":"Subject + haven't / hasn't + V3: She hasn't finished yet."},{"label":"Present Perfect","title":"Question","subtitle":"Have / Has + subject + V3? Have you ever seen this film?"},{"label":"Present Perfect Continuous","title":"Affirmative","subtitle":"Subject + have / has been + V-ing: I have been studying."},{"label":"Present Perfect Continuous","title":"Negative","subtitle":"Subject + haven't / hasn't been + V-ing: He hasn't been sleeping well lately."},{"label":"Present Perfect Continuous","title":"Question","subtitle":"Have / Has + subject + been + V-ing? How long have you been waiting?"}]}

{"title":"3. Where the focus changes the tense","sections":[{"label":"Result now","color":"blue","description":"Choose Present Perfect when the action is complete and the ready result, count, or fact matters now. just, already, and yet often point to this focus.","examples":[{"en":"I have just cleaned the kitchen.","ua":"The kitchen is clean now."},{"en":"Have you written the email yet?","ua":"We ask if the finished result is ready by now."}]},{"label":"Experience up to now","color":"violet","description":"Choose Present Perfect for life experience with ever and never when the exact past time is not important.","examples":[{"en":"Have you ever visited London?","ua":"We ask about life experience up to now."},{"en":"I have never ridden a horse.","ua":"We talk about no experience until now."}]},{"label":"Process and duration up to now","color":"emerald","description":"Choose Present Perfect Continuous when the process or length of time matters. for, since, how long, lately, and recently often appear here.","examples":[{"en":"I have been studying since 7 o'clock.","ua":"The process started earlier and continues up to now."},{"en":"How long have they been waiting?","ua":"The question is directly about duration."}]},{"label":"Same situation, different focus","color":"amber","description":"The context can be similar, but the meaning changes: a ready result now or an activity up to now.","examples":[{"en":"I have painted the room.","ua":"The room is ready now."},{"en":"I have been painting the room for two hours.","ua":"The activity has been going on up to now."}],"note":"Present Perfect often answers What has happened?, while Present Perfect Continuous often answers What have you been doing? or How long?"}]}

{"title":"4. Typical mistakes","items":[{"label":"Mistake 1","color":"blue","title":"Using Present Perfect when the process is not finished and duration is the main idea","wrong":"❌ I have painted the wall since 10 o'clock.","right":"✅ I have been painting the wall since 10 o'clock."},{"label":"Mistake 2","color":"emerald","title":"Mixing a continuous form with a finished result","wrong":"❌ I have been finished my homework.","right":"✅ I have finished my homework."},{"label":"Mistake 3","color":"violet","title":"Using continuous with stative verbs","wrong":"❌ I have been knowing him for years.","right":"✅ I have known him for years."},{"label":"Mistake 4","color":"amber","title":"Using continuous for a finished number or count","wrong":"❌ I have been writing three emails today.","right":"✅ I have written three emails today."}]}

{"title":"5. Stative verbs and an important note","items":["know, like, believe, understand, want, and need usually stay in Present Perfect, not in Present Perfect Continuous.","Say I have known her for years, not I have been knowing her for years.","for and since do not automatically mean continuous: with states we often choose Present Perfect, for example We have lived here since 2020.","For A2–B1, keep the simple rule: state = Present Perfect, activity or process = Present Perfect Continuous."]}

{"title":"6. A short decision guide","items":["Do you need a ready result, a number, or a finished fact now? → choose Present Perfect.","Do you have just, already, yet, or life experience with ever / never? → usually choose Present Perfect.","Do you have how long?, for / since, or an activity that continues up to now? → choose Present Perfect Continuous.","Do you need a recent process with a visible result now? → choose Present Perfect Continuous.","If the verb is stative, stay with Present Perfect even when the time period continues up to the present moment."]}

{"title":"7. Pages and related topics in this block","items":[{"label":"Tenses","url":"/en/theory/tenses","current":false},{"label":"Present Perfect","url":"/en/theory/present-perfect","current":false},{"label":"Present Perfect Continuous","url":"/en/theory/present-perfect-continuous","current":false},{"label":"Present Perfect vs Present Perfect Continuous","url":"/en/theory/tenses/present-perfect-vs-present-perfect-continuous","current":true},{"label":"Past Perfect","url":"/en/theory/past-perfect","current":false},{"label":"Past Perfect Continuous","url":"/en/theory/past-perfect-continuous","current":false}]}
